mnemonics.title: Dijkstra's Algorithm Steps
mnemonics.content: Dijkstra's can be remembered as: 'I Set Infinite Distance, Then Grab Ugly Vertex, Relax Neighbors' (Initialize, Set source=0, Grab minimum, Update neighbors).

mnemonics.title: Relaxation Rule
mnemonics.content: Remember: 'If going this way is cheaper, take it' - if dist[current] + edge_weight < dist[neighbor], then update.

mnemonics.title: When Dijkstra Fails
mnemonics.content: Negative weights break Dijkstra because 'Greedy locks the best' - once a vertex is finalized with minimum distance, negative edges could still improve it.

acronyms.expansion: Shortest Path Tree - the tree formed by shortest paths from source to all vertices
acronyms.usage: The output of Dijkstra's algorithm is an SPT rooted at the source vertex

acronyms.expansion: Single-Source Shortest Path - problem that Dijkstra solves
acronyms.usage: Dijkstra solves the SSSP problem for graphs with non-negative weights

acronyms.expansion: Extract-Min and Decrease-Key operations are essential for efficient Dijkstra
acronyms.usage: Using a priority queue gives O((V+E) log V) complexity vs O(V²) with arrays

visualTips.visualization: Picture a seesaw: once you lock a vertex as 'final', imagine a negative edge creating a path that goes 'under' the locked vertex's distance
Think of the priority queue as a stack of papers sorted by distance - always take the top (smallest distance) paper first
Imagine updating a shorter route like updating your GPS when you find a faster path - the distance 'relaxes' to a better value

analogies.analogy: Like choosing the nearest unvisited city on a trip - you pick the closest one now, not considering if a longer route might later lead to faster overall completion
Like a hospital triage system - patients (vertices) are prioritized by urgency (distance), and the most urgent (minimum distance) is always served first
Like a family tree but for routes - each vertex has a 'parent' that led to its shortest path from the source ancestor
